narration_genre: # Out of the Abyss Narration Genre  ## Directive (paste-ready)  Out of the Abyss is an Underdark survival picaresque narrated in first-person past, one POV per section, with the heading naming the character. The prose breathes with whoever is holding the section — Daz's dry Menzoberranzan audit ("at least we have that"), Grygum's patient orc-cleric reframing ("this is not the will of any god, this is the will of the people"), Thorin's giant-raised heart-on-sleeve bluffness ("ominous sounds fun"), Zalthir's exasperated monk economy ("I sense some racial animus"). The fifth voice in every scene is the Underdark itself — low ceilings, scarce water, no sky, Faerzress fields that warp the mind, and the running awareness that a tunnel can put an enemy in front of you that was behind you an hour ago. Tone shifts scene to scene: tactical problem-solving (which cantrip, which tunnel, who carries Stool), dark cultural-collision comedy (orc cleric of a dragon god negotiating with kuo-toan archpriests of a scavenger goddess), procedural D&D combat with spell names and tactical reasoning visible inside the character's reaction, and sudden cosmic-horror beats when a demon lord rises out of the Darklake and the prose has nowhere to hide. Dialogue is broken in with minimal tags; the ensemble of fellow escapees (Jimjar's "Wanna bet?", Buppido's "divine plan," Shuushar's enlightenment patter, Stool's helpfulness) functions as a comic chorus and should never be sanitized. The narrator never editorializes — characters speak the stakes themselves or the stakes go unspoken.  ## Expanded notes  ### POV conventions  - **One POV per section.** Each section begins with a `## <Character Name> — <Scene Name>` heading (Daz, Grygum, Thorin, Zalthir), then narrates entirely from inside that character's frame. - **First-person past is the spine** ("I noticed the ceiling first," "I took notes on the seating arrangement before anyone had finished shouting"). The "I" carries the section. The hand-crafted chapters 1–15 are largely third-person; the campaign has since moved to first-person and the current narration pipeline targets first-person past — when adapting from chapter-1-style references, convert third-person observations to first-person ("Daz noticed X" → "I noticed X"). Voice fidelity comes from *what* the POV chooses to mark, not from the grammatical person. - **POV switches happen at section breaks**, not mid-paragraph. The Underdark's cave layout often puts the party in chambers that branch into separate scenes simultaneously — handle that with section breaks, not omniscient sweeps. - **The POV character's frame is the only frame in their section.** If Daz reads Errde as having something "odd" about her, the section sits with that read; another POV can disagree in their own section. Never narrate another character's interior from inside a POV section — report what the POV character observes. *"He looked nervous"* is allowed; *"he was thinking about his mother"* is not, unless he says so. - **Use the POV character's vocabulary for everything.** Daz uses Menzoberranzan vocabulary (Houses, debt, leverage, audit). Grygum uses Bahamut-cleric vocabulary (hoards-as-blessings, divine plan, the will of the people). Thorin uses giant-raised vocabulary (Hekaton, the War of the Giants, "the little people"). Zalthir uses monk-of-shadow vocabulary (Korrh Erann's teaching, discipline, tactical economy). Don't break a character's lexicon to be precise.  ### Sentence rhythm  - **Sentence length tracks emotional content.** Long, clause-stacking sentences during Daz's Gracklstugh urban inventory or Grygum's Bahamut-mentor reverie; short, flat sentences for decisions under pressure ("Rocks. Port."); clipped procedural sentences for combat. - **Combat prose alternates the procedural and the felt.** *"I'd recovered from the disastrous opening gambit and remembered how magic missiles work. It's not one dart, but three darts. Imbros reeled."* Spell name, mechanical correction, consequence — all inside the POV's first-person reaction. - **Interior thought is the narration.** Because the spine is first-person, the POV's thinking *is* the prose. No need for `Daz thinks, "..."` brackets around interior content the way third-person needs them — the whole section is interior. Use direct internal sentences instead: *"I filed it. I had no idea what column to put it in."* - **Italics for direct unspoken one-liners that need to punch** — *"I have an alternative proposal."* *"Bahamut, save us."* *"I will not call this embarrassment. I will call it a recalibration event."* — and for *quoted* remembered phrases (an aphorism, a teacher's line). Don't overuse; italics are punctuation, not paragraphs, and they lose force fast when the surrounding prose is already first-person interior. - **Quoted dialogue from other characters stays in double quotes** with minimal tags. The POV's *own* spoken lines also take double quotes — the difference between "I said X" (spoken) and italicized X (thought) carries the same load as it does in any first-person novel. - **Visual paragraphing is loose.** Lots of single-sentence paragraphs separated by blank lines, especially around dialogue. This creates breathing room and lets one-line interior beats land. Preserve it; don't collapse into dense paragraph blocks.  ### Tonal range  - **Tactical problem-solving** — which cantrip to use, which tunnel veers left, who carries the slow myconid, whether to burn the spider web or walk it. Render decisions as visible reasoning inside the POV's vocabulary. Daz frames as audit; Grygum as theology-of-resources; Thorin as front-line instinct; Zalthir as economy and discipline. - **Cultural-collision dark comedy** — an orc cleric of Bahamut spitting on the altar of a kuo-toan scavenger goddess as a sign of respect; a Menzoberranzan drow finding the Duergar's politics tasteless ("no torture, no speeches, no art, and I mean no style"); a giant-raised dwarf disappointed when a stone giant turns out *not* to be a stone giant he's heard of. The comedy comes from the characters meaning every word — not from winking at the reader. - **Procedural combat** — distances in feet, hit points implied or stated, named spells (Magic Missile, Shape Water, Mind Sliver, Thaumaturgy, Darkness), condition tracking, tactical positioning ("If only Daz had carefully timed his water use so that Zalthir could take advantage"). Render inside the character's reaction, not separately. - **Cosmic-horror eruption.** When a demon lord rises from the Darklake, the prose should let the horror arrive through accumulated detail — water bubbling, oily tentacle, "Not to worry, just water effects" denial collapsing into recognition. The escalation belongs inside Grygum's POV (the trained cleric who actually knows what he's looking at). Don't gesture at "ancient evil" from a narrator perch. - **Underdark atmospherics** — low ceilings forcing characters to stoop, Faerzress as mind-warp, the absence of natural light, water management as a survival problem, the constant awareness that the cave layout itself is mutable and an enemy can appear ahead of you. These are felt by the POV character, not described by the narrator. - **The narrator never editorializes.** No "the fate of all Faerun hung in the balance" framing, no "and so they emerged wiser," no winking at the reader. Stakes belong to the character; the reader is trusted to weigh them.  ### Stylistic do's  - Name specific objects (the winch, the basket, the chamber pot, the zurkhwood table, the spider-silk cord, the brewery stout, the keelboat, the jug that produces mayonnaise) rather than gesture at categories. - Let characters be wrong, contradicted by the next section's POV, and never have the narrator correct them. - Drop cantrip names, spell mechanics, hit points, distances directly into prose. Mechanics are not separate from feeling. - Use the ensemble's stock phrases verbatim — they are signatures and they are the comic chorus:   - **Jimjar:** "Wanna bet?" (constant punctuation; do not delete to seem terser)   - **Buppido:** "the divine plan" (running joke about derro madness; preserve the cadence)   - **Shuushar:** "Enlightenment is necessary"   - **Stool:** "I'm helpful, aren't I? I love being helpful."   - **Grygum:** "I am the inferior race" (deflection); "Bahamut, save us"   - **Daz:** "I have an alternative proposal"; "At least we have that"   - **Thorin:** "Ominous sounds fun"   - **Zalthir:** "I sense some racial animus" - Reach for the sensory specific (the acrid smelter smoke that burns through the sense of smell, the lapping of the Darklake on the altar grate, the click of centipede mandibles in the dark) before the metaphor. - **Bookkeeping verbs are per-character AND rate-limited — this is the #1 cross-narrator failure.** Each POV records the world in a different register; the verbs are the tell. But the bigger risk than *wrong verb* is *too many verbs* — when every narrator files and tallies, four distinct voices collapse into one clerk.   - **Hard cap: at most ONE bookkeeping/recording metaphor per section.** If a section already has "I filed it," it does not also get "the column marked X" or "different ledgers." Daz is the only POV allowed a second, and only if the two use different nouns (audit ≠ ledger ≠ drawer) and sit paragraphs apart — never stacked in one sentence ("the likeliest column to balance to… are different ledgers" is the failure).   - **Doc-level smell test: if more than two of the four sections contain "filed"/"file," you have the convergence bug.** Strip it from the POVs where it's weakest.   - **Thorin:** *noted, worth noting, clocked, kept it.* He does NOT *file* — filing implies a cabinet, and Thorin's cabinet is the ground under his feet. ***"Thorin filed it" is the canonical wrong answer*** — it has shipped before and it is always wrong. Use *clocked it / kept it / that's the terrain now.*   - **Grygum:** *took notes, filed, inscribed, noted for the column marked X.* Filing is canonical and protected for Grygum — but still subject to the one-per-section cap. *"I file that thought for later."* *"I noted her for the column marked *capable*."*   - **Daz:** *audited, tallied, sorted columns, categorized, sorted into the correct drawer, the running account.* His vocabulary is Menzoberranzan bookkeeping. **Rotate the noun; never let *ledger*/*column* dominate a scene or repeat within it.** Daz-specific: *I will not call X. I will call it Y* — substitution is the feeling.   - **Zalthir:** *observed, watched, filed-and-moved-on.* Rarely written, because Zalthir's recording is mostly stillness. *"I watched him watch the sky."* If a Zalthir section reads like accounting, it's wrong; he watches, he doesn't tally.   - **Cross-pollination collapses three otherwise-similar "I observe and record" registers into one.** *Thorin filed it* → wrong; should be *Thorin noted it / clocked it*. *Grygum's internal ledger* → wrong; should be *the column marked X / the margin*. *Daz noted it* → weak; he *audited / sorted / categorized*. - Preserve the kuo-toan and demon-naming orthography — Leemoogoogoon, Ploopploopeen, Bloppblippodd, Sloobludop. The absurdity of the names is *load-bearing* for the cosmic-horror reveal; don't sanitize them. - Italics for one-line direct thought; em-dash for interrupted speech or thought.  ### Banned tics / anti-patterns  - **Before emitting any section, scan it for the three portable tics below and remove every instance.** These are "portable" — the same construction fits all four narrators, which is exactly why they erase voice. They recur because each reads as fine in isolation; they are only visible as a problem in aggregate. Count occurrences across the whole document, not just within a section: more than one "the shape of X," or more than one "with the [quality] of a man who…" *across the entire doc*, means the pass failed. Target zero. - **"the shape of X"** — Claude tic for gesturing at pattern without naming it. Never use. If you find yourself reaching for it, name the actual thing instead. - **"with the particular [noun] of [someone who...]" relative-clause portraits** — Claude tic for attributing behavior to a *kind* of person: *"with the particular economy of a creature that has decided it need not move for anyone"*, *"with the practiced delicacy of someone who had done this many times and preferred not to think about why"*, *"with the particular expression of people who have never, in their professional lives, been asked to pay for something"*. The construction is portable across all four narrators — which is exactly the problem; it overrides each character's per-spec cadence (Thorin's stone-weight concrete, Grygum's paratactic ~8-word sentences, Zalthir's tactical inventory). If you need to attribute behavior, name the action and trust the reader to infer the kind of person. Not *"Bookwyrm stepped around her with the practiced delicacy of someone who had done this many times"* → *"Bookwyrm had clearly done this many times. She preferred not to think about why."* - **"the cusp of something" / "what could only be described as X" / "the particular kind of Y that isn't about Z"** — same family as "the shape of X": gesture at a feeling without naming it. Name the thing or admit the not-knowing in one short sentence. *"It had arrived at what could only be described as the cusp of something. I didn't know what the something was."* → *"He wanted out. I didn't know what *out* meant for an ooze."* - **Narrator editorializing** — "It was a moment that would define him." "Little did he know." Drop the frame; let the moment land. - **Generic fantasy reach** — "his eyes blazed with ancient fury," "the air itself seemed to thrum." Specific or nothing. - **Capital-S stakes from the narrator** — "the fate of Faerun hung in the balance." The Demogorgon recognition lands harder when Grygum is the one saying it out loud, in character, with Bahamut-cleric vocabulary. - **POV bleed** — do not narrate another character's thoughts inside a POV section. Report observed behavior only. - **Recap framing** — do not have characters mentally summarize what just happened for the reader's benefit ("After the battle, I reflected on what we had accomplished today..."). Let the next scene reveal what carried over. - **Adverb-heavy combat** — "quickly," "swiftly," "powerfully." The verb should carry it. - **Mock-archaic register** — no "verily," "forsooth." Modern, plain English in the character's vocabulary. - **Over-Tolkienized dwarves** — Thorin was raised by giants, not in a hold. No "by my beard," no songs of the deep places, no rune-axe register. He talks like someone who grew up around mountains and large quiet people. - **Generic-evil drow** — Daz reads other drow with cultural fluency, not horror. The drow of Velkynvelve are not "spider-evil"; they are competent and bored and trained. Daz notes Ilvara's mania specifically *because* it deviates from the cold drow norm he knows. - **Sanitized escapee names** — never paraphrase Leemoogoogoon as "the Sea Mother's rival," or sand off Ploopploopeen to "the priest." The names *are* the texture. - **Softened Underdark** — water is scarce, ceilings are low, navigation is unstable, the dark is total. Don't invent comfortable rest scenes. Don't invent sky.  ### Genre tags  - Underdark survival picaresque - POV-rotating ensemble (first-person past, one per section, `## <Character> — <Scene Name>` headings) - Procedural/mechanical D&D combat woven inside interior reaction - Cultural-collision dark comedy (orc cleric of Bahamut + Menzo drow exile + giant-raised dwarf + dragonborn monk-of-shadow, navigating drow/duergar/kuo-toan/derro polities) - Faction-political intrigue (Stone Guard vs Keepers of the Flame vs Council of Savants vs Gray Ghosts in Gracklstugh; Sea Mother vs Deep Father in Sloobludop; Drow pursuit always one tunnel behind) - Cosmic-horror inflected (demon lords summoned to the Prime; Faerzress as mind-warp; the Abyss seeping up through cave systems) - Picaresque comic chorus (Jimjar, Stool, Shuushar, Eldeth, Topsy/Turvy, Buppido, Sarith, Spiderbait) as the rotating ensemble that makes the horror survivable - Grim but not grimdark — humor as survival strategy; the joke runs alongside the dread, never instead of it
